title: The Macro Minute: Can the market continue looking through adverse trade policy outcomes?

Happy Monday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dell to
present our macro minute for Monday,
August 11th, 2025. Hope everyone had a
great weekend. So, as always, we'll
start with the executive summary from
today's leadoff morning note. If you
would like to review the analysis
supporting these conclusions and to see
if you need to make any changes in your
portfolio to remain on the right side of
market risk or to get on the right side
of market risk to the extent you haven't
been following Kiss and Dr. Mo, you need
to be a client of 42 macro. So today's
schematic question is can the market
continue looking through adverse trade
policy outcomes? Our answer Nvidia and
advanced micro devices agreed to pay 15%
of their Chinese AI chip sales revenue
to the US government in exchange for
export licenses. Nvidia declined 1% and
AMD declined 2% on the news. The deal
aligns with US President Donald Trump's
push to secure direct financial gains
for America in trade negotiations,
echoing Apple's $600 billion US
manufacturing pledge while also
representing an unprecedented targeted
export tax in modern corporate history.
The quidd pro nature of the arrangement
may be challenged in courts and it
certainly undermines the national
security rationale for the export
controls. Thus far, Wall Street has
largely overlooked companies making
pledges to support the administration's
economic agenda as the cost of doing
business during a Trump presidency. We
can envision a scenario whereby the
market starts to push back on the
increasing entanglement of the
government and private sector,
especially considering the relationship
element of it, i.e. kiss the ring, get
the green. Elsewhere, the domestic
economic calendar is light on quantity
but heavy on quality from an economic
signaling perspective. By the end of the
week, we'll have a much better sense of
how tar of how tariffs and trade policy
uncertainty are impacting the economy
from a stagflation perspective. We'll
also learn how tariffs are impacting the
federal budget balance as well as how
the passage of the one big ugly bill
impacted the economy from a reduction of
uncertainty perspective. Transitioning
to our 42 micro dashboard, as always, I
wrap up with a question from our
community. This one's titled inflation
risk versus growth outlook. Says, uh, hi
Darius. Hi everybody. I'm a new 42 macro
subscriber from Europe and I found your
work invaluable for navigating market
risk. Thank you. I appreciate that kind
words. Says, "Right now, I'm concerned
that tariffs and deportations and
slowing immigration could drive an
upswing in inflation as the attached
recent chart from Apollo suggests just
as growth momentum is weakening in such
a stagflationary setup. I struggle to
see the Fed cutting rates anytime soon.
Obviously, this would be bad for risk
assets. I hope I'm wrong." Uh so, uh our
our thoughts on this, you know, I think
we've been pretty clear about uh you
know, the economy is in a U-shaped
economy. We do have inflation uh
trending higher over the medium term.
We've been consistent with that as
relates to our uh uh sticky inflation
view. Um with regards to inflation,
however, I think it's very important to
think about, you know, taking a step
back when you're always thinking about
when you're thinking about growth and
inflation specifically, there's really
three things that matter to asset
markets. The delta, so what's the
direction of travel for growth or
inflation? Uh it's the surprise risk,
what you know, what's it being reported
relative to consensus expectations. And
then there's the level uh specifically
you know if we have below trend growth
or above trend growth that tends to have
different outcomes create different
outcomes in asset markets or if we have
above target inflation or below target
inflation for regards to the central
bank's target that also creates
different conditions and in and across
asset markets. And so uh with regards to
growth uh we have growth slowing in
terms of our good model projections and
our fundamental research projections you
know two separate processes kind of
arriving at the same conclusion. We have
growth slowing but likely to continue
generally and persistently surprising
consensus expectations to the upside as
evidenced by you know uh what we talked
about on Friday with regards to um or
not on Friday I want to say on Tuesday
or Wednesday of last week with regards
to the um the positive uh surprises we
continue to see from corporate earnings.
I want to say through Friday we had 453
of 497 S&P 500 companies having reported
earnings through Friday and I want to
say there was a 60 uh no so like a 81.2%
2% beat rate for earnings. That's the
highest beat rate we've seen on earnings
since like the the second half of 2021.
Uh and then I think we have like a
62-ish percent beat rate on sales.
That's also the highest beat rate on
sales we've seen since 2021. I mean four
years ago, you know, I mean, and so, you
know, the companies are beating and
surprising consensus estimates to the
upside. Uh we suspect the same is likely
to continue to occur with regards to
growth, broader economic growth, even
though we're still slowing in the
context of the US-shaped economy. on
inflation. We have inflation
accelerating, but consensus has
inflation accelerating at the same pace
as our model. Uh, and so it may be the
case that inflation just comes in in
line. And if it comes in in line, you
know, the market's already expecting the
acceleration. And so as is the Fed. And
so if we continue to see growth slowing
despite, you know, the the the
confirmation that inflation's in fact
accelerating, I think the Fed will still
uh it would be wise for the Fed to to
focus on growth because it is a leading
indicator of the inflation cycle as
opposed to managing monetary policy with
backward looking data for the lag most
lagging indicator of the business cycle.
So, I do believe that we're going to
start seeing rate cuts uh in in
September and maybe even again in
October, but more than likely another
one by December despite the acceleration
in inflation because again uh I think
now that we have Dr. Steve Moran joining
the Fed board uh I think with the the
his entire job for the next, you know,
three to four, five months is to
convince everybody else on the Fed board
that tariffs aren't inflationary. And
obviously he's one of the most brilliant
academics um from he has one of the most
brilliant minds from an academic
economist perspective in the world. So
if anybody can get there and talks some
sense in these folks uh it'll probably
be him and if if he can't do it then
then god help us all. So we'll wrap it
up there. Terry here presenting our
macro minute for uh Monday August 11
2025. Best of luck out there today.
We'll catch you back here tomorrow.
Cheers.
